text: Three trocars are placed in the lateral abdomen, including a five millimeter optical trocar for initial intraperitoneal access. The abdominal cavity is insufflated to fifteen millimeters mercury for laparoscopic inspection. A twelve millimeter trocar is placed under direct vision, followed by an eight millimeter robotic trocar in the left lower quadrant. The five millimeter optical trocar is replaced with an eight millimeter robotic trocar. The omentum is reduced to expose two hernia defects: an umbilical hernia on the left and an incisional hernia from a previous laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

The surgeon defines the extent of the hernia and incises the peritoneum at least five centimeters proximal to the hernia defect. A preperitoneal flap is developed, similar to techniques used in tap inguinal hernia repairs. The preperitoneal fat is reduced from the umbilical hernia defect.

The surgeon continues the peritoneal flap dissection distal to the hernia defect, switching from a thirty-degree up to a thirty-degree down angle for better visualization. The dissection is performed meticulously to preserve the integrity of the peritoneum. After creating an adequate flap for mesh placement, the surgeon uses OV-lock sutures to close the incisional hernia defect, followed by closure of the umbilical hernia defect with a second suture.
The surgeon incorporates a bite of subcutaneous skin into the suture repair to recreate the inverted umbilicus. Small disruptions in the peritoneum are re-approximated using 3-0 Vicryl suture.

The assistant guides the Ethicon Physiomesh into the intraperitoneal cavity. The mesh is secured to the abdominal wall using the Ethicon secure strap tacker. The peritoneum is re-approximated with the secure strap stapler.
